column10: Cognitive Flexibility (Magical Thinking 10, Rigid hyperlogical thinking 0)
column18: Empathy (Affective) (Over-absorbing others’ pain 10, Low affective empathy 0)
column29: Sense of Self (Rigid identity 10, Diffuse identity 0)

column2: Emotional Predator (Sandra L. Brown) or PrimaryPsychopath
column5: Highly calculating and targets partners for utility rather than connection. Performs vulnerability or distress strategically to manipulate perception while remaining emotionally detached internally. Thrilling early on but destabilizing over time.

column2: The Terrorist (Lundy Bancroft), Sociopath, or Secondary Psychopath
column5: Rules through intimidation and fear. Uses threats, rage, and punishment to enforce obedience. Keeps partners hyper-vigilant and afraid to act independently. Small perceived slights trigger disproportionate reactions. Maintains control by making the partner afraid of consequences.

This type lives in a world where everything revolves around them. They demand constant validation and react with rage or withdrawal when criticized. They present a grandiose exterior, but it often hides fragile self-esteem. Gaslighting, triangulation, and subtle put-downs are common tools. Relationships revolve around keeping the narcissist supplied with admiration.

column2: The Drill Sergeant (Lundy Bancroft), Maybe OCD
column5: Rigid and domineering, enforces strict rules on daily life. Micromanages routines, decisions, and emotional expression. Disobedience is met with shaming framed as discipline. Control extends into finances, chores, and roles. Partner feels infantilized and stripped of autonomy.

Constantly suspects betrayal or hidden plots. Misreads neutral behavior as threatening and demands reassurance or proof of loyalty. Keeps relationships tense through suspicion and monitoring. Mistrust isolates both partner and self from outside support.

Positions himself as the ultimate authority on every subject. Dismisses and talks over others until they stop challenging him. Treats disagreement as disrespect rather than dialogue. Being right matters more than fairness or connection. Over time, partners begin doubting their own judgment.

Resists requests or intimacy indirectly through procrastination, forgetfulness, or silent sabotage. Avoids saying no directly but undermines outcomes until others give up. Appears compliant on the surface yet creates tension through inaction. Words and actions don’t match, eroding trust and shifting responsibility onto others.

column2: The Clinger (Sandra L. Brown), Borderline Personality Disorder
column5: Overly enmeshed and terrified of abandonment, demands constant reassurance and closeness. Interprets boundaries as rejection and escalates neediness when distance is perceived. Dependence feels flattering at first but becomes suffocating. Pressures partner into emotional caretaker role rather than equal.

Addiction dominates priorities and destabilizes relationships. Substances or compulsive behaviors override commitments. Cycles through promises, guilt, charm, and relapse. Partners manage crises, finances, or excuses while trust erodes through repeated instability.

Seeks a partner to function as caregiver rather than equal. Expects indulgence, reassurance, and forgiveness while avoiding responsibility. Uses charm and helplessness to shift burdens onto the partner. Creates a parent-child dynamic that builds resentment as the partner carries both roles.

Hooks partners with grand future promises—marriage, travel, plans—but never follows through. Uses fantasy as bait to maintain investment. When confronted, deflects or invents obstacles. Cycles between hope and disappointment, keeping partners waiting and doubting themselves.

Impulsive, distractible, and novelty-driven, brings energy and spontaneity but struggles with follow-through and organization. Focus shifts quickly, leading partners to feel overlooked or dropped unintentionally. Inconsistency reflects attention regulation differences rather than intent. Stability improves with structure, support, and understanding.

Literal, direct, and less attuned to social nuance, with focused interests and strong internal logic. Emotional reciprocity may appear limited due to different processing rather than malice. Can be steady, honest, and loyal when understood; shutdowns/meltdowns may be misread as coldness.

Undermines partner’s emotions and perceptions by minimizing or mocking them. Frequently dismisses feelings as overreactions and frames themselves as rational authority. Chronic invalidation creates loneliness and self-doubt until the partner stops expressing emotions altogether.

Charming but inconsistent, keeps intimacy just out of reach. Shows warmth when convenient, then withdraws behind excuses, busyness, or emotional walls. The push–pull dynamic creates longing and uncertainty, leaving partners sustained on intermittent attention rather than real commitment.

Seductive, dramatic, and thrilling, overwhelms partners with attention and passion, then withdraws once pursuit fades. Drawn to novelty and conquest rather than stability. Creates emotional highs followed by sudden absence, leaving partners hooked on intensity but deprived of real commitment.

column2: The Perpetual Victim (Lundy Bancroft), Covert Narcissist
column5: Casts themselves as the wronged party in nearly every situation and deflects responsibility by blaming others. Uses sympathy as leverage to avoid consequences and recruit rescuers. Emotional oversharing and shifting stories create instability, leaving partners drained, guilty, and stuck in caretaker roles.
